title: What to know about Perilla frutescens (shiso) before using it medicinally
intro_desc: Shiso, scientifically known as Perilla frutescens, is a highly revered herb that boasts numerous health benefits, ranging from reducing inflammation to fighting off free radicals. In culinary circles, shiso is prized for its delicate flavor and aroma, often used in Japanese cuisine to add depth to soups, salads, and sushi. Horticulturally, shiso is an easy-to-grow perennial herb that thrives in partial shade and well-drained soil. Botanically speaking, shiso belongs to the mint family and features fragrant leaves with a distinctive flavor profile. With roots dating back to ancient China and Japan, shiso has been a staple ingredient in traditional medicine for centuries, earning it a reputation as a prized addition to any herbal repertoire.
section_medicine_ailments: Perilla frutescens helps healing several common ailments, such as bronchitis, asthma, and allergies, thanks to its potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The herb has been traditionally used in Eastern medicine for centuries to treat respiratory issues, and modern studies have confirmed its efficacy in reducing inflammation in the lungs and airways, making it an effective natural remedy for bronchitis and asthma sufferers. Additionally, Perilla frutescens has also shown promise in alleviating symptoms of allergies, including itching, sneezing, and congestion, by inhibiting the release of histamine and other inflammatory chemicals from immune cells. Furthermore, its antibacterial properties have been found to help combat sinus infections and other upper respiratory tract infections, making it a valuable addition to natural cold and flu remedies. Overall, Perilla frutescens is a versatile and effective herb that can be used to promote respiratory health and alleviate symptoms of various common ailments.
section_medicine_properties: Perilla frutescens has several medicinal properties, such as anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial activities, which contribute to its widespread use in traditional medicine. The most important property of Perilla frutescens is its ability to reduce inflammation, which makes it effective in treating various inflammatory-related diseases, including arthritis, gout, and allergic reactions. Its potent antioxidant activity also helps protect the body against oxidative stress and cell damage caused by free radicals, thereby reducing the risk of chronic diseases such as cancer, cardiovascular disease, and neurodegenerative disorders. Additionally, Perilla frutescens has been shown to exhibit antimicrobial properties, making it effective in treating infections caused by bacteria, viruses, and fungi. Its antibacterial properties have also been found to inhibit the growth of Helicobacter pylori, a bacterium associated with stomach ulcers and gastric cancer. Overall, the medicinal properties of Perilla frutescens make it a valuable natural remedy for maintaining overall health and treating various diseases.
section_medicine_parts: The most commonly used parts of Perilla frutescens for medicinal purposes are the leaves, stems, and seeds. The leaves, in particular, have been extensively studied for their bioactive compounds, including flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenes, which have been shown to possess potent anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties. These compounds have been found to contribute to the herb's traditional uses in treating various health conditions, such as fever, rheumatism, and respiratory issues. The stems of Perilla frutescens are also used medicinally, with their essential oil rich in sesquiterpenes, which exhibit anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities. Additionally, the seeds of Perilla frutescens contain a unique fatty acid composition that is believed to contribute to its traditional use as a treatment for digestive issues and respiratory problems. Overall, the various parts of Perilla frutescens have been found to possess a range of bioactive compounds that support its long-standing reputation as a valuable herbal medicine in traditional Asian cultures.
section_medicine_side_effects: When used improperly, Perilla frutescens increases the chances of experiencing side effects, such as allergic reactions, gastrointestinal distress, and interactions with medications. For instance, taking large doses of Perilla frutescens supplements without consulting a healthcare professional can lead to an allergic reaction, manifesting in symptoms like hives, itching, and difficulty breathing. Additionally, consuming Perilla frutescens in large quantities or for extended periods may cause gastrointestinal issues such as nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and stomach cramps due to its high polyphenol content. Furthermore, Perilla frutescens has been known to interact with certain medications, including blood thinners, diabetes medications, and blood pressure medications, which can lead to adverse reactions or decreased efficacy of these drugs. In extreme cases, improper use of Perilla frutescens may also cause more severe side effects, such as kidney damage, liver toxicity, or even an increased risk of bleeding complications. It is essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using Perilla frutescens for medicinal purposes to minimize the risk of experiencing these unwanted side effects.
section_medicine_precautions: The precautions to take before using Perilla frutescens medicinally are crucial, as this herb can interact with certain medications and have adverse effects in some individuals. One of the most important precautions is to consult with a healthcare professional or licensed practitioner before taking Perilla frutescens supplements, especially if you are pregnant, breastfeeding, or have a pre-existing medical condition, such as high blood pressure, diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. Additionally, it is essential to monitor your blood sugar levels closely when using Perilla frutescens, as it may lower glucose levels and interact with medications used to treat diabetes. Furthermore, individuals taking anticoagulant medications should also exercise caution, as Perilla frutescens may increase the risk of bleeding due to its ability to thin the blood. Moreover, patients undergoing surgery or having a medical procedure should stop using Perilla frutescens at least two weeks prior to their scheduled appointment, as it may interfere with blood clotting and anesthesia. By taking these precautions, individuals can minimize potential risks and ensure safe and effective use of Perilla frutescens for medicinal purposes.
section_horticulture: Perilla frutescens, a species of perilla native to East Asia, requires specific horticultural conditions to thrive. This annual or perennial herb grows best in well-draining soil with a pH between 6.0 and 7.0, rich in organic matter and moderate in fertility. It prefers full sun to partial shade, with temperatures ranging from 65°F to 85°F (18°C to 30°C), although it can tolerate some frost. The plant requires adequate moisture, particularly during the germination and seedling stages, but also appreciates good air circulation to prevent fungal diseases that thrive in humid environments. In terms of watering, Perilla frutescens benefits from regular but not excessive irrigation, with about 1-2 inches (25-50 mm) of water per week being sufficient. Additionally, this versatile herb responds well to pruning and pinching, which encourages bushy growth, promotes leaf production, and helps control its tendency to become leggy. By providing the right combination of soil, light, temperature, moisture, and care, gardeners can successfully cultivate Perilla frutescens in a variety of settings, from containers to beds, and enjoy its distinctive flavor and aroma in their cooking.
section_horticulture_growth: Perilla frutescens, a member of the mint family, has specific growth requirements to thrive. This annual herb prefers well-draining soil with a pH between 6.0 and 7.0, making it adaptable to a range of garden environments. In terms of temperature, Perilla frutescens thrives in full sun to partial shade, tolerating temperatures between 65°F (18°C) and 85°F (29°C). It requires moderate moisture levels, with around 1 inch (2.5 cm) of water per week, but is susceptible to root rot if the soil is too wet or waterlogged. To promote healthy growth, Perilla frutescens benefits from regular pruning to encourage bushy growth and prevent flowering. Additionally, it can be grown in containers as small as 6 inches (15 cm) deep, making it an ideal choice for urban gardens or small spaces with limited soil depth. With proper care and attention to its specific needs, Perilla frutescens can produce vibrant foliage and delicate flowers that attract pollinators, adding a unique charm to any garden or container arrangement.
section_horticulture_planting: Perilla frutescens, also known as shiso or Korean basil, is a popular herb that requires specific planting conditions to thrive. To start, choose a location with well-draining soil and full sun to partial shade, as direct sunlight can cause the leaves to become scorched. Perilla seeds are typically sown directly in late spring or early summer when the soil has warmed up to at least 60°F (15°C). Sow the seeds about 1/4 inch deep and 6-8 inches apart in rows that are 12-18 inches apart, then water gently but thoroughly. Keep the soil consistently moist during the first few weeks after germination, as Perilla seedlings can be sensitive to drought. As the plants grow, thin them out to about 12-18 inches apart to prevent overcrowding and promote healthy air circulation, which helps prevent fungal diseases that thrive in humid environments. With proper care, Perilla frutescens will grow rapidly, producing attractive red or purple leaves with distinctive veins and a flavorful, slightly bitter taste that adds depth to many Asian-inspired dishes.
section_horticulture_caring: Perilla frutescens, also known as Korean basil or shungiku, is a popular herb that requires proper care to thrive. To ensure optimal growth and prevent pests and diseases, it's essential to provide Perilla frutescens with the right environment and attention. Firstly, choose a location with partial shade to full sun and well-draining soil that is rich in organic matter. Water the plant regularly, but make sure not to overwater as this can lead to root rot. Fertilize lightly during the growing season using a balanced fertilizer, and prune the plant regularly to encourage bushy growth and prevent it from becoming leggy. Additionally, inspect your plants frequently for signs of pests or diseases such as aphids, whiteflies, and fungal infections, and take prompt action if necessary. Finally, protect your Perilla frutescens from extreme temperatures and frost by bringing potted plants indoors during the winter months or using a cold frame outdoors. By following these caring tips, you can enjoy the delicate flavor and aromatic fragrance of this lovely herb for years to come.
section_horticulture_harvesting: Perilla frutescens, also known as shungiku or Chinese chrysanthemum, is a popular herb in many Asian cuisines. When it comes to harvesting, timing is crucial to ensure optimal flavor and aroma. The best time to harvest Perilla frutescens is when the leaves are young and tender, typically 2-3 inches long. Avoid picking older leaves as they can be bitter and lose their delicate flavor. To harvest, simply pinch or cut off individual leaves at the stem, leaving a small piece of stem intact to allow for continued growth. Harvesting in the morning, after the dew has evaporated but before the heat of the day, is also recommended as this allows for better retention of essential oils and flavors. Additionally, handle the leaves gently to avoid bruising or tearing, which can cause the herb to become bitter and lose its aroma. With proper harvesting techniques, Perilla frutescens can be enjoyed in a variety of dishes, from salads and soups to stir-fries and teas.
section_horticulture_pests_diseases: Perilla frutescens, a popular herb widely cultivated for its leaves, seeds, and flowers, is susceptible to several pests and diseases that can significantly impact its growth and productivity. One of the most common pests affecting perilla is the aphid, which feeds on the plant's sap and can cause curled or distorted leaves. The caterpillar of the tobacco hornworm is another significant pest, as it feeds on the leaves and stems of the plant, causing damage and reducing yields. Additionally, the fungus-like organism, powdery mildew, can infect perilla, covering its leaves in a white powder that reduces photosynthesis and impairs growth. Root rot caused by fungal pathogens such as Pythium and Rhizoctonia is another significant disease, which can be spread through contaminated soil or water, causing plant mortality if left unchecked. Other diseases affecting perilla include leaf spot caused by fungi such as Colletotrichum and Septoria, as well as bacterial leaf blight caused by Xanthomonas.
section_botany: Perilla frutescens, a species of plant belonging to the mint family (Lamiaceae), is characterized by its distinctive foliage and growth habits. The plant is an annual or perennial herb that can grow up to 2-3 feet tall, with a sprawling habit and a tendency to spread out along the ground. Its leaves are the most striking feature, being large, oval-shaped, and deeply lobed, with wavy margins and a soft, slightly fuzzy texture. The leaves are typically dark green on top and lighter green or whitish-green on the bottom, with a distinctive purple tint around the edges. The plant produces small, inconspicuous flowers that are clustered in whorls along the stem, which are often hidden by the foliage. Perilla frutescens also has a distinctive root system, featuring a taproot and lateral roots that can spread several feet from the parent plant. Overall, the botanical characteristics of Perilla frutescens make it a visually striking and easily recognizable species in the garden or in the wild.
section_botany_taxonomy: Perilla frutescens belongs to the family Lamiaceae, which is commonly known as the mint or deadnettle family. Within this family, P. frutescens is classified under the genus Perilla, which consists of about 30 species of flowering plants native to Asia and Eastern Europe. The specific epithet "frutescens" indicates that it is a type of shrub or small tree, as opposed to other members of the genus that are herbaceous or annual.
section_botany_variants: Perilla frutescens has variants, such as Deulkkae, which is the most widely cultivated and consumed type in Korea, known for its strong fragrance and slightly bitter taste. The other variant is Shungiku, also known as Tuberose-leaved Perilla, which is native to China and Japan, characterized by its delicate flavor and sweet aroma. There are also some hybrid varieties that have been developed, such as the Red Perilla, which has a deeper red color and a slightly sweeter taste than Deulkkae. These variants differ in their appearance, taste, and fragrance, but all share the same nutritional benefits and culinary uses.
section_botany_distribution: Perilla frutescens, also known as shiso or Korean perilla, is a perennial herb native to Asia. Its geographic distribution includes China, Japan, Korea, and parts of Southeast Asia such as Taiwan and Indonesia. It has been naturalized in some areas and is now grown in other parts of the world with warm climates, including the southern United States, Australia, and Africa.
section_botany_life_cycle: Perilla frutescens, also known as shiso, is an annual or perennial plant native to East Asia. Its life cycle typically begins in early spring when seeds germinate and seedlings emerge. The plants grow rapidly, reaching maturity within 60-90 days, and produce delicate white, pink, or purple flowers. After pollination, the flowers develop into pods containing numerous seeds, which mature and dry over the next few weeks. Once the seeds are dry, the plant typically dies back, marking the end of its life cycle. However, perilla can also be propagated by cuttings, allowing it to regenerate and start a new cycle.
section_history: Perilla frutescens, also known as Korean basil or shiso, has a rich history dating back over 2,000 years. In ancient China and Korea, it was used as an herbal medicine to treat various ailments, including digestive issues and fever. In Japan, it was used as a culinary herb and in traditional tea ceremonies. The leaves and seeds were also used in rituals and offerings to the gods, symbolizing good luck and prosperity.
section_history_mythology: Perilla frutescens, also known as Korean basil or wild sesame, has rich mythological references in East Asian cultures. In Chinese mythology, it is associated with the legend of the "Divine Child," who was sent to earth by the gods to bring prosperity and good fortune. In Korean folklore, perilla leaves are said to have been used as a sacred offering to the goddess of childbirth, easing labor pains and ensuring safe delivery. These mythological references highlight the plant's significance in ancient cultures, symbolizing fertility, abundance, and protection.
section_history_simbology: Perilla frutescens, also known as shiso, holds significant symbolic meanings in East Asian cultures. In Japanese and Korean traditions, it is a symbol of good luck, prosperity, and longevity, often given as gifts to new couples or business partners. Its vibrant red leaves are said to represent passionate love, while its green leaves signify harmony and balance. Additionally, Perilla frutescens is believed to ward off evil spirits and bring peace to the home.
section_history_literature: Perilla frutescens, also known as Korean basil or shungiku, has a rich history that dates back thousands of years. Historical texts such as the "Shennong Ben Cao Jing" (Divine Farmer's Herb-Root Classic) from China around 200 BCE and the "Samguk Sagi" (History of the Three Kingdoms) from Korea around 1000 CE, mention Perilla frutescens for its medicinal properties and use in traditional cooking. It was also mentioned in ancient Japanese texts as a staple herb in Japanese cuisine.
section_history_artifacts: Perilla frutescens, a herb native to East Asia, has a rich history dating back thousands of years. Historical artifacts related to Perilla frutescens include ancient Chinese and Korean ceramics depicting its leaves and flowers, as well as tomb paintings from the Han Dynasty (206 BCE - 220 CE) showing its use in traditional medicine. The Mawangdui Silk Textiles, a set of silk garments buried with the wife of a Han dynasty general, also feature intricate designs of Perilla frutescens.
